AC.name: Access / cost
top_examples:
For people with a BKN amputation this type of leg is overkill and the comfort loss is not worth the benefits of this style.   These silicone socks are torturous and a sweaty mess when you wear them for just a couple hours.

I wear a leg with a foam insert and a sock.  I can take it on or off in 2 seconds and i can wear it all day without getting uncomfortable and stinky/sweaty.  

The problem is that you cant hardly find a prosthetist that has the skills anymore.  The ones nowadays just take measurements and send it off to be manufactured somewhere else so the try to set every client the same.  

So if you are new to being an amputee you wont even know the old style leg exists.

I should reiterate the silicone sock style is great for people with no knee left and it is difficult for the leg to stay on.  But if you have a knee the leg with lock on without weird silicone socks or belts.
Someone out there help us amputated people in Guam.Guam only has one prosthetic maker but they don't take Medicare Therefore we cannot afford prosthetic limbs.

!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!QUESTION!!!!!!!!!!!!!I had my left leg amputated just below the knee in Aug of 2023. I am now about to make my first appointment for a prosthetic, it seems you have a considerable amout of knowledge concerning the pros and cons of different prosthetic.  My question is is one company preferable. Akso which type of prosthetic do you prefer.

My brother lost his leg same as yours ,he was 23 years old ,had a Wife ,3 Children and never went on Disability,this was in the 70s . No insurance paid for his prosthetic leg or socks. His wife worked to pay for everything and bills and food. 
He started working after 8 months as a mechanic with cars. He worked through the fathom pains and the blisters ,and never complained. Your lucky to have a fancy one. Good Luck.
My Brother has passed away now at 76 , he worked right up till he passed. 🕊️🌹🇺🇸

i get where youre coming from with becoming a walking advertisement without much choice in the matter.. but unlike tattoos on skin you can easily cover up or customize your prosthetic without the commitment of a tattoo cover up, put some paint or stickers over the logos (if you change your mind they can be peeled off or painted back over easily), get creative with how you dress it up or even sand down non-structural parts to be rid of the logos if theres nothing legal barring you from that

either way, if the companies are helping you to be able to walk even if youre paying full price for the prosthetic then i dont see too much issue in having a couple logos on it

AL.name: Activity limitation
top_examples: Terry fox’s artificial leg was something, he had like 4 legs when he was running across Canada and I’ve seen the leg he ran on in Ottawa at the museum of Canadian history, it was something to see and I was impressed, all it was straps and springs
Same here. The remedy is to squirt liquid soap between your skin and the rubber sleeve. Once the prosthetic is off, then WD40 the mechanism. I got sand in there after a day at the beach.

When worked as a Ride Host at Cedar Point amusement park Summer of 2007, I was loading people onto Shot side (red)* of Power Tower, securing harnesses, asking them to remove visible loose items to set on the tray until they get back down, etc., and making banter.

Someone handed me their sunglasses, I spun around to put them on the shelf, and there was a leg on it. 🦿 I know I must've at least froze for a moment, if not outright jumped. Yes, a leg gave me a jump scare. 😅 

It was late October and *_finally_* the weather had turned guests attire from multi layer pastel tank tops and ugly shorts (pastel yellow with the other pastels in a pattern) to From Russia With Love. The only thing that didn't change were the oversized sunglasses. 😎

So everyone had gotten on the ride with jeans and the appearance of two legs, so it caught me off guard, especially since it took up the whole shelf.

Once I'd cleared and launched them, the control tower calls me on our station phone ☎️  _Is that a leg?_ Yup. 😅

*Shot side (red) shoots riders 240 feet in the air, and then slowly lowers them. 

Drop side (aqua) raises riders 240 feet in the air, drops them about 2/3rds of the way, yo-yo's them back up part way (and it's at this moment that the most phones, that they weren't supposed to bring on the ride in the first place, go flying out of pockets ((Summer 2007 it was Motorola Razr))), and then slowly lowers them.

P.s. Our rider numbers for that summer were lower than usual because on a similar kind of vertical ride at Kentucky (iirc) Six Flags, a cable snapped and made some girl/woman (I don't know her age) into a double amputee, taking at least both her feet. 😢 [I'm going to go look it up.]

P.p.s. I had a rider come up the exit ramp for special loading due to disability. He was a double amputee at the knees. He'd already ridden Raptor, and our harnesses were a similar over the shoulder, presses to the thighs, design, so good to go!

🤔💭  _Right after he left the ride afterwards, I thought, _I wonder if he bought any Raptor merch that said, I kicked the sky, for the irony factor._ 😅😂

I had my right leg amputated but I don't call myself an amputee. All this new socket tech is ok unless you live in the tropics. Towelling stump socks are the best for this suitation. Now fifty five years later, someone might say, "How are you going?" my reply is still running, jumping, climbing trees and jumping out of helicopters. Cheers

Jo, the only thing I can think is that the guy couldn't wrap his mind around you dancing.  Prosthetics have gotten so much better and compared to the 80's where it appeared that they were mainly about appearing like the missing part today's prosthetics seem like they're much more about functionality over how they look.  In my career discussing prosthetics is treated like any other health issues, usually it's about the best places to call for help if your prosthetic leg goes out during a hike.  Now days I fight fire , a fire fighter I work with on occasion has a prosthetic leg and a prosthetic arm, just a little extra awareness towards him just the same as any other firefighter with a medical condition.  Great guy, very solid FF.  Always happy to have him on scene.

CT.name: Control (upper limb)
top_examples: Please try the Ottobock negative vacuum system, it’s the only one that has been comfortable for me and not resulted in any skin irritation or pinch points with fantastic proprioception.  It has the same carbon fiber socket as the pin system, but uses an inner and outer liner that form a vacuum around the limb and socket with a small pump in the toe keeping everything nice and tight with no need to use any socks.  It has been life changing for me, no more skin irritation or nerve spikes (except when I get a viral infection like a flu, but that’s different, right?), and the liners last me a year without wearing out and slipping off.

I met a stranger at work the other day, prosthetic arm and leg (opposite sides). I hardly even noticed, and about halfway through his interaction, he asked me if I was okay, which caught me a little off guard. I told him I was fine, and asked why he thought I might not be. He told me that fir the last decade, his prosthetics are all anyone has ever been able to focus on when it came to interactions with him. I shook my head and assured him I hadn't really registered the prosthetics, and didn't give a flying fuck either way. I told him that I'd served, and that was why. Little things like that just didn't really matter to me. He looked like he was almost in tears, asked about my service and all in all, he told me he had an amazing appointment and would definitely be coming back. The worst part? I'm a GP, a doctor.

There was an incident where a climber was accused of cheating, and I can kind of see the point, less weight is a definite advantage with climbing, as long as the grip and mobility are there with the prosthesis. but at the same time it's very much unfair and discriminatory to say, "you're disqualified on account of all the work you put in to get back to surpass your previous skill level after regaining mobility following that horrific chainsaw accident!"  That would not be cool.

The thing none of these people are getting is that maybe the real advantage an amputee has is that they've lost that limb, and know what life is like without it, after all it's not like you're walking again in 6 weeks, like with a broken leg.   Maybe these athletes have just found more motivation to work harder than the other athletes.

@metawarp7446 Prosthetics aren't integrated into the nervous system, so you don't get the fine autonomous control over your joints or the same range of motion, or any of the other things our meat limbs generally do (for example, I have all my meat limbs, but osteoarthritis means I don't have all the cartilage in my knees I should, and my extensor tenosynovitis in my right wrist means it's prone to painful inflammation, but they're still much more functional than any prosthetic). That's why you need different prosthetics for different purposes; a walking leg is better for, y'know, general walking, but it doesn't have the energy return of a running blade, let alone what our tendons do in our meat legs.

Most people in the modern world don't do regular endurance running, but one of the things we have over most other species is the potential for utterly _absurd_ stamina; there are a few populations that live in ways similar to what we evolved doing, and they can run down birds. As in, the birds can't fly as long and as far as humans can without rest. We have used horses for transportation for tens of thousands of years, but their advantage isn't stamina, it's weight capacity. A human can move at pace for distances and times that would kill any horse. And we're also far more resilient by comparison; we have evidence of people healing from pretty severe injuries (and being cared for by their group) even before we had agriculture.

@FootlessJo Im praying for you to make a speedy recovery. You are an inspiration to me. You seem to be very strong. Ive had 4 surgeries on my lower back, and am constantly in moderate to severe pain. In addition, i am slowly losing my vision. I havent been able to drive the past 5 years, because outside during the day, im snowblind. I am blessed to have the best wife in the world. She keeps me motivated, and doesn't seem to mind being my chauffeur.  There were two things I was going to recommend, but you have done one. They are snow skiing and Scuba. I have worked with Telluride adaptive sports. They ski with me, serve as a good pair of eyes and keep me from further injury. They are great with my paraplegic and amputee friends. The second is Scuba. They say there's healing in the water. You can google HSA, and they can help you get certified and find volunteers where you like to go to the beach. I prefer south florida. I dive with a group of disabled veterans, and there is even a quadriplegic who loves to dive with us. It takes 3 volunteers to make that happen. One holds her tank and propels her. A second keeps eye contact with her, and with different blink signals she can tell him to pinch her nose so she can equalize, and several other signals. She lives for opportunities to dive or hang out with friends. It will seem like no time and your stroke will be behind you. Never give up and get back to doing what you love to do. You have a lot of fans praying for you. God bless.

DD.name: Don / doff
top_examples: Someone out there help us amputated people in Guam.Guam only has one prosthetic maker but they don't take Medicare Therefore we cannot afford prosthetic limbs.

Get a vinyl cutter and you can make your own decals and “tattoos” whenever you want! You can even remove and replace them easily if you decide to change them.
I have a cricut and I love it… I make my own decals for everything. I don’t have a local friend who uses a prosthetic or I’d offer to make them whatever they want. Although I could always just mail it to someone far away…

Well that is what differentiates products from companies. I get that you feel weird about it, but let’s say (hypothetically) you are walking down the street with your leg and you meet an amputee who would be able to walk, but just doesn’t have a prosthetic device. It would be difficult to memorize where each part of your leg came from, so having the labels might not just be for the companies. I don’t know the first thing about owning prosthetics so please correct me if I’m wrong, but the company logo might not just be for the companies.

I understand.  The same happened to me when I had stopped for lunch after getting a new socket.  Fortunately I was still close to
My prosthetist’s office and could just walk in and get the button re attached.  People who don’t deal with prosthetics do not understand the costs and the real dependence we have on our equipment

As a non-amputee, I can clearly state that if something like that happened to me, I'D DO THE SAME THING. It's basically a car (expense wise) on the end of your nubbin, except without it, you're not walking. You don't go fiddling with things if one wrong move can cause your ankle to just...not work. Good job on being smart and the good content!

EP.name: Emotional / psychological
top_examples:
😅 As if these need advertising!? "Wanna loose a limb? Get your prosthetic HERE!" 😂🤦‍♀️🤦‍♀️
I would so write over that. Make funny pictures out of this.
But..your ankle... actually looks like your signature. Like a F and a J in one. Footless Jo.😂 Which I just saw, cos my sister's name starts with a J and her new husband's name starts with an F and they have a similar sign edged into their wedding rings 😅
Wasn't always that way. Go back 30 years and prosthesis had a more cosmetic look.  There is NO need  for  labeling medical devices....like a billboard.

It's frustrating to me when people decide to be rude to a creator like you! Someone could have gotten the same information politely by asking why you elected to call your prosthetist before trying to release it with an Allen key... We still get an additional informative follow-up video, and you don't have to deal with rude comments

Ohm. Curious how often are prosthetic limbs stolen? 

A friend had hers stolen at her physical therapy group. 2 other people have lost theres in the last 4months

Can I ask something really stupid?


Why are they so expensive?

Like, I've seen some prosthetics that have lights, sensors, bells and whistles galore, but from what I've seen of yours (which admittedly is very little) it kind of just looks... leg shaped. 

Like, there's probably a hinge-joint thing at the ankle, maybe there's something flexible about the foot, but other then that... I mean, I could see it getting much more complicated if your amputation is above the knee, but I don't think yours is....?

Anyways, I guess I'm saying that it kind of seems like a fancy peg leg. I feel like (if I knew how to carve) I could take a log and carve it into something leg-shaped enough to work relatively well. Then just put some sort of thing in for the ankle, and boom! Prosthetic leg!

I'm sure it's much more complicated than that, I'm just not sure why ir would be.

HS.name: Heat / sweat
top_examples: I agree with your response…. I work as an OA at a Hanger Clinic….. until your in that would you may not get it.  The number of calls that come in weekly for needing new sleeves.. hot spots from rubbing, warranties on some of the prosthetic components, weight loss, change in your physical activity and the list is ongoing. It is a very rewarding job. The level of you relationships with your patient is incredible . I can’t say enough about how happy it makes me to watch a patients progression. Thank you for sharing your experience.

It has the same carbon fiber socket as the pin system, but uses an inner and outer liner that form a vacuum around the limb and socket with a small pump in the toe keeping everything nice and tight with no need to use any socks. a locking prosthesis is a horror for me when you work in it for a long time in the cold, sweat and moisture get into the lock and freeze when you want to quickly take it off because your leg hurts from long movements and you can’t because the moisture in the lock has frozen

Be careful when choosing a light source. Most radiate a lot of heat. Heat rash sucks, burns suck more. My favorite prosthetic leg was a war vet who had a blacklight scene of the Greatful Dead (think black light poster for the 90"s). That guy always has the coolest legs! I grew up around a V.A.M.C on the nursing home side. So nobody had all the orginal pieces.

MG.name: Mobility / gait / falls
top_examples: To be fair, most clothes are also a walking advertisement. Like a nike t-shirt and shoes for example. Although a prosthetic is apart of your body i see it in a similar way to how clothes are advertised

I understand wanting to advertise your product, but isn’t this a really niche market?? Like how often are you walking down the street and have another random amputee come up and going “wow is that a Flower ankle? How do you like it? I just got a Tacten one last week that feels pretty alright”

i get where youre coming from with becoming a walking advertisement without much choice in the matter.. but unlike tattoos on skin you can easily cover up or customize your prosthetic without the commitment of a tattoo cover up, put some paint or stickers over the logos (if you change your mind they can be peeled off or painted back over easily), get creative with how you dress it up or even sand down non-structural parts to be rid of the logos if theres nothing legal barring you from that

either way, if the companies are helping you to be able to walk even if youre paying full price for the prosthetic then i dont see too much issue in having a couple logos on it

It would be difficult to memorize where each part of your leg came from, so having the labels might not just be for the companies. I don’t know the first thing about owning prosthetics so please correct me if I’m wrong, but the company logo might not just be for the companies.

Are you kidding me thats how expensive a prosthetic leg is .my my you could purchase a house for that money.
We do owe God a lot of money .most people are walking around on legs for free. isn't God good .a leg for thousands of dollars .

PM.name: Pain (mechanical)
top_examples:
For people with a BKN amputation this type of leg is overkill and the comfort loss is not worth the benefits of this style.   These silicone socks are torturous and a sweaty mess when you wear them for just a couple hours.

I wear a leg with a foam insert and a sock.  I can take it on or off in 2 seconds and i can wear it all day without getting uncomfortable and stinky/sweaty.  

The problem is that you cant hardly find a prosthetist that has the skills anymore.  The ones nowadays just take measurements and send it off to be manufactured somewhere else so the try to set every client the same.  

So if you are new to being an amputee you wont even know the old style leg exists.

I should reiterate the silicone sock style is great for people with no knee left and it is difficult for the leg to stay on.  But if you have a knee the leg with lock on without weird silicone socks or belts.

I agree with your response…. I work as an OA at a Hanger Clinic….. until your in that would you may not get it.  The number of calls that come in weekly for needing new sleeves.. hot spots from rubbing, warranties on some of the prosthetic components, weight loss, change in your physical activity and the list is ongoing. It is a very rewarding job. The level of you relationships with your patient is incredible . I can’t say enough about how happy it makes me to watch a patients progression. Thank you for sharing your experience.

I’m having a below the knee amputation September 3 three days from now. I’ve had five surgeries on his right foot ankle replacement a year ago that didn’t work out. I’m pretty tough, but that ankle replacement was very painful. How painful is this? Gonna be the amputation the day after surgery? God bless you and hope you’re doing well.

I wonder if it was made with the same method/system in mind, but with more than just 1pin in the center, if that would help with the pain, discomfort, and the overall weight distribution issues you pointed out...hmmm.
Say instead,  the socket was equipped with 3 or 4 weapons – i mean pins – laid out in a certain configuration that helped distribute the weight more evenly across the full surface area of the bottom of the nubbin.

... 
Is that maybe already a thing that exists?
If not.... is there a reason that wouldn't work out as I'm imagining?!!?

I WAS AN A/K RIGHT LEG AMPUTEE SINCE 1971. WITHIN A FEW YEARS, I PULLED MY LEG ON AND OFF FOR LIKE 38 YEARS. HAVE TO SAY, THERE WAS NEVER A DAY WHEN I DIDNT FEEL PAIN AND DISCOMFORT WITH EVERY STEP. THAT THAT DOES NOT KILL YOU, MAKES YOU STRONGER. ITS NOT FAIR, BUT IT IS WHAT IT IS.😅😅😅

PP.name: Phantom pain
top_examples: I love your art work and I do understand your phantom pain. I suffer from that also. This is going to sound strange for sure, but you are very sexy. When I see your reels all I see is your beautiful face.
I've been wondering how you're doing these days; IIRC you were dealing with some aggravating issues like phantom pain and other stuff. Has life gotten any better for you?

This got me thinking (very unrelated)
If some people get phantom sense in VR on parts of their character that they don’t have (wings, tails, horns, etc.) how does that work for an amputee playing VR? Is it the same sort of thing?

I have a question, how do you personally think a person would deal with having two amputated legs instead of just one?
I’m asking bc I have a character named Sera Matthews who lost both of her legs to gangrene when she was young, and this channel has been helping me get ideas for a book I’m planning on writing about her (like including phantom pain)

I saw a video you made about phantom pain, and i have a question regarding it.

Since its kind of a placebo effect in pain form, could you, in theory, take something for the pain and have it actually help? I apologize in advance if its a dumb question, but its the first thing that came to mind when i saw you mention phantom pains

SF.name: Socket fit / volume
top_examples: I hadn't even considered this 😮 I'm going to be fitted for my first test socket next month to join the unwittingly enlisted brand advertising corps. 🤔🙃🤨

For people with a BKN amputation this type of leg is overkill and the comfort loss is not worth the benefits of this style.   These silicone socks are torturous and a sweaty mess when you wear them for just a couple hours.

I would so write over that. Make funny pictures out of this.
But..your ankle... actually looks like your signature. Like a F and a J in one. Footless Jo.😂 Which I just saw, cos my sister's name starts with a J and her new husband's name starts with an F and they have a similar sign edged into their wedding rings 😅

My brother lost his leg same as yours ,he was 23 years old ,had a Wife ,3 Children and never went on Disability,this was in the 70s . No insurance paid for his prosthetic leg or socks. His wife worked to pay for everything and bills and food. 
He started working after 8 months as a mechanic with cars. He worked through the fathom pains and the blisters ,and never complained. Your lucky to have a fancy one. Good Luck.
My Brother has passed away now at 76 , he worked right up till he passed. 🕊️🌹🇺🇸

This really doesn’t seem like too big of a deal, electronics all have their company logos on em, your car has its company logo on it, your clothes have their company logo on the tags, why would a prosthetic be any different? It may be on your body, but it is a product made by a company that has a right to put their logo on what they make. If it really bothers you, just cover it with paint, stickers, tape, or simply wear clothes over it.

SF_UL.name: Sensory feedback
top_examples:
This doesn't even remotely compare since it's not... a *replacement body part* and necessary for daily functioning, but I was gifted a few really old sewing machines from someone who knew I wanted to learn but couldn't remotely afford the equipment on my own. Even though I could see several moving parts and intuit I could do something to them, I didn't know how to use the equipment and didn't feel comfortable fiddling with anything until I had read the manuals on what those parts did and how to use them properly. Not because I couldn't figure it out with common sense, but because it's so easy to break something if you don't know exactly what it's supposed to do. If that something is a thing you got for $10 off of Amazon and is easily replaceable, that risk isn't really a problem. But if it's something you can't remotely afford to replace or repair (especially if replacement parts might not even be made anymore), you darn well aren't going to try to force something and just hope it doesn't break.

Another example: I don't think it's quite the same level as a prosthetic limb (for me at least, since I have one ear that hears fairly well, so I could hear at all if this happened), but I do use a hearing aid. There's a certain amount of 'oh CRUD' that comes when something you rely on like one of your own body parts just breaks. Panic, trying to figure out if you can replace it if it's hosed (most insurance companies do not see things like hearing aids or CIs or prosthesis or motorized mobility aids or such as things necessary for functioning but on the level of priority to someone's health as a medically unnecessary plastic surgery, some don't cover them at all or only cover them partially and so you must pay sometimes prohibitively expensive amounts of money, and are very strict about when they will cover you getting replacement parts and what parts they will cover), thinking ahead and calculating how difficult things you have planned for both necessity like a job, or leisure and fun and just *living* will still be possible... It's very scary. But for me when stuff like that happens, the panic is mixed with this cold dread that just freezes me and forces me to slow down, because I know that in a crisis, sometimes nobody else is going to come and help me, so I have to collect myself and proceed with utmost caution and not lose my head - though admittedly that has happened more with serious injuries than my hearing aid for me personally. But for a family member I have who is deaf without hearing aids and has a CI, I've seen that same freaking out when something goes wrong, because their job and ability to socialize and pretty much everything they do relies on being able to hear. And a friend of mine could not afford even arm crutches when they found they would need them for basic mobility - it was only the kindness of someone from our friend group *in another country* ordering them crutches secretly as a surprise that made it possible for them to have that aid which allows them to function... at a fraction of the ease of someone who's well, and still with pain and difficulty just from existing.

I guess the point I'm getting at is trying to help others who might not understand how it feels, to have some empathy. It isn't just equipment, it's almost like a part of your body, and there is such a huge pay wall between the people who direly need them, and basic functions that the majority of people have simply by virtue of being alive. If something was stuck around your biological leg and you were genuinely worried you'd break your leg possibly irreparably if you got it off wrong, you'd absolutely proceed with caution and ask someone who's an expert on what's stuck on your leg for help. Especially if one wrong move could put you out of commission for months and lead to really expensive hospital bills just to get your leg to work again, or just have to go without walking.

I hope none of this came off as aggressive or bitter towards anyone. I don't hold ire towards *anyone* for being healthy, I think everyone deserves to be as healthy as they possibly can. But learning empathy and compassion for people with different needs can help possibly anyone you meet. Adding extra time between seeing someone struggling, and some kind of value judgment or judgment of their competency or intelligence or whatnot, opens more room for connection and compassion rather than division and kicking someone who's already down. Even if it's in small ways, like a comment that might not have been meant to cut as deep as it does simply because someone didn't think a few moments longer about what they were saying and how it would affect someone else. It's hard enough not to internalize stuff like feeling bad about yourself for struggling, even when you know it's not your fault.
Newly dating any suggestions on how to start after 35 yrs of marriage and 4 yrs amputation to left leg and5 finger tips not feeling feminine

Please try the Ottobock negative vacuum system, it’s the only one that has been comfortable for me and not resulted in any skin irritation or pinch points with fantastic proprioception.  It has the same carbon fiber socket as the pin system, but uses an inner and outer liner that form a vacuum around the limb and socket with a small pump in the toe keeping everything nice and tight with no need to use any socks.  It has been life changing for me, no more skin irritation or nerve spikes (except when I get a viral infection like a flu, but that’s different, right?), and the liners last me a year without wearing out and slipping off.

I have not had a stroke, but I have been through so much as well and I wish I could give you a hug. 

I am 35 years old and a triple amputee thanks to flu A & septic shock. I lost my second foot, which had previously been reconstructed with a flap from my back, on 4/3 due to a 1+ year battle with osteomyelitis. I have five fingers on my left hand and no other digits or notable extremities. (I was in perfect health prior to 12/22. I am also a wife and mom of 7, and barely 35.) 

I am only commenting because I relate to your difficulty right now when it comes to asking for help. We were self-reliant for so long, but right now we are at rock bottom. Asking for help is so hard. So vulnerable. I am lucky to be alive. WE are lucky to be alive. 

I know you are swamped with fans reaching out, but I wish I could give you a hug. ❤ If you have a spare moment, and Facebook, please feel free to reach out to me. I don’t know anyone like me. (No pressure, but my account is fb/aliconcha) Take care of yourself! You are an inspiration!!

Wow, What a horrible thing to go through,  especially this young! I will never feel sad about my dimple BKA due to diabetes and sepsis! You really went through the wringer! No pun intended at all! Your great spirit and attitude really encourage me to get out of my wheelchair and try to do more.. I only went through the chop chop on my lower leg a month ago so I'm still getting used to the wheelchair and driving with my left foot..The hard thing is to get the heavy Medline style wheelchair into my side minivan door (Chrysler Pacifica) as it weighs a lot.. After a week of driving now I have built up my arm strength to lift it up with one try instead of 3 or 4 try's! It is hard! I hope to get measured for a prosthetic as soon as my stump heals. I have fallen twice on my stump, once in rehab and once at home on carpet in my bedroom so maybe it will take longer..How long did it take you to get a prosthetic leg? Did you go into rehab? I wish you the best of luck! I'm in FL too..east of Tampa in Brandon area..Watched your shower video..Now I really do feel old using a shower chair!!

SK.name: Skin / hygiene
top_examples:
GET A DERMA ROLLER (the wheel thing that has the really tiny needles? THAT). Yeah, I know we are told not to shave and what not because micro abrasions and possibility of infection, but if you use the roller at night before sleep, and wash the liner daily, you should be good to go. I've even actually used a liner straight through for 48 hours without problem (removing once ever 6 hours for 15 minutes to let the skin breathe).

The derma roller stimulates the healing response in your skin and tricks the skin to grow thicker. It'll make wearing the pin lock system A LOT MORE BEARABLE.

I've been using the pin lock system for about a year, and IMO it's the second best next to getting an osseointergration surgery (I'm working on that one... my buddy lost his arm overseas, got the surgery. He wears his arm all day every day). I use the pin lock system for my leg, and I can do muay thai and firearm training as well as I used to before losing the leg. 

The only downside is that the liner I have (Willowwood Alpha Classic) is simply not durable enough to deal with the abuse I put it through. I have 4 on standby at all times (not a cheap endeavor) that insurance didn't pay for, simply so that I can rest assured I'll be ready when one wears through.

Please try the Ottobock negative vacuum system, it’s the only one that has been comfortable for me and not resulted in any skin irritation or pinch points with fantastic proprioception.  It has the same carbon fiber socket as the pin system, but uses an inner and outer liner that form a vacuum around the limb and socket with a small pump in the toe keeping everything nice and tight with no need to use any socks.  It has been life changing for me, no more skin irritation or nerve spikes (except when I get a viral infection like a flu, but that’s different, right?), and the liners last me a year without wearing out and slipping off.

If you want, i could crochet you a lace stocking that goes over your prosthetic leg and part of your original leg? If I do it in cotton it should be nice and durable, plus you could just toss it in the wash on whatever setting when it needs it

I had no idea this girl I was talking to had a prosthetic (she always wore pants) and my legs were sore. I made the comment on the phone to her "my legs are killing, feels like they're gonna fall off." 

She didn't talk to me after that. I found out through a mutual friend she thought I was making fun of her.

Be careful when choosing a light source. Most radiate a lot of heat. Heat rash sucks, burns suck more. My favorite prosthetic leg was a war vet who had a blacklight scene of the Greatful Dead (think black light poster for the 90"s). That guy always has the coolest legs! I grew up around a V.A.M.C on the nursing home side. So nobody had all the orginal pieces.

SV.name: Service / training
top_examples: Too bad that advice from the doctor/manufacturer isn’t given when you receive the prosthesis you know “here’s what you do if it doesn’t release. You can’t be the only one that has happened to.

This doesn't even remotely compare since it's not... a *replacement body part* and necessary for daily functioning, but I was gifted a few really old sewing machines from someone who knew I wanted to learn but couldn't remotely afford the equipment on my own. Even though I could see several moving parts and intuit I could do something to them, I didn't know how to use the equipment and didn't feel comfortable fiddling with anything until I had read the manuals on what those parts did and how to use them properly. Not because I couldn't figure it out with common sense, but because it's so easy to break something if you don't know exactly what it's supposed to do. If that something is a thing you got for $10 off of Amazon and is easily replaceable, that risk isn't really a problem. But if it's something you can't remotely afford to replace or repair (especially if replacement parts might not even be made anymore), you darn well aren't going to try to force something and just hope it doesn't break.

Another example: I don't think it's quite the same level as a prosthetic limb (for me at least, since I have one ear that hears fairly well, so I could hear at all if this happened), but I do use a hearing aid. There's a certain amount of 'oh CRUD' that comes when something you rely on like one of your own body parts just breaks. Panic, trying to figure out if you can replace it if it's hosed (most insurance companies do not see things like hearing aids or CIs or prosthesis or motorized mobility aids or such as things necessary for functioning but on the level of priority to someone's health as a medically unnecessary plastic surgery, some don't cover them at all or only cover them partially and so you must pay sometimes prohibitively expensive amounts of money, and are very strict about when they will cover you getting replacement parts and what parts they will cover), thinking ahead and calculating how difficult things you have planned for both necessity like a job, or leisure and fun and just *living* will still be possible... It's very scary. But for me when stuff like that happens, the panic is mixed with this cold dread that just freezes me and forces me to slow down, because I know that in a crisis, sometimes nobody else is going to come and help me, so I have to collect myself and proceed with utmost caution and not lose my head - though admittedly that has happened more with serious injuries than my hearing aid for me personally. But for a family member I have who is deaf without hearing aids and has a CI, I've seen that same freaking out when something goes wrong, because their job and ability to socialize and pretty much everything they do relies on being able to hear. And a friend of mine could not afford even arm crutches when they found they would need them for basic mobility - it was only the kindness of someone from our friend group *in another country* ordering them crutches secretly as a surprise that made it possible for them to have that aid which allows them to function... at a fraction of the ease of someone who's well, and still with pain and difficulty just from existing.

I guess the point I'm getting at is trying to help others who might not understand how it feels, to have some empathy. It isn't just equipment, it's almost like a part of your body, and there is such a huge pay wall between the people who direly need them, and basic functions that the majority of people have simply by virtue of being alive. If something was stuck around your biological leg and you were genuinely worried you'd break your leg possibly irreparably if you got it off wrong, you'd absolutely proceed with caution and ask someone who's an expert on what's stuck on your leg for help. Especially if one wrong move could put you out of commission for months and lead to really expensive hospital bills just to get your leg to work again, or just have to go without walking.

I hope none of this came off as aggressive or bitter towards anyone. I don't hold ire towards *anyone* for being healthy, I think everyone deserves to be as healthy as they possibly can. But learning empathy and compassion for people with different needs can help possibly anyone you meet. Adding extra time between seeing someone struggling, and some kind of value judgment or judgment of their competency or intelligence or whatnot, opens more room for connection and compassion rather than division and kicking someone who's already down. Even if it's in small ways, like a comment that might not have been meant to cut as deep as it does simply because someone didn't think a few moments longer about what they were saying and how it would affect someone else. It's hard enough not to internalize stuff like feeling bad about yourself for struggling, even when you know it's not your fault.

Ohm. Curious how often are prosthetic limbs stolen? 

A friend had hers stolen at her physical therapy group. 2 other people have lost theres in the last 4months

Real talk.  After an accident in 2017, I suffered major foot and leg injuries.  My ankle was broke off at the joint, and the heel detached, with many bones out of the skin and broken/misplaced.  The pain is unbearable a few days a week.  Does the pain from the amputation where the prosthetic goes on feel that way too?  I really am contemplating amputating the foot off as it is full arthritic and doesn’t even have 50% of use.  It’s hard to talk to a doctor about it who never experiences the pain or has had an amputation.  I am open to Any suggestions

WD.name: Weight / durability
top_examples: I had the same thought when I very first got my prosthetic and I asked the the people who where I got my prosthetic from could I like either paint over it or wrap it and they told me it would mess with my warranty so I had to keep it like that but I truly understand what you're saying

I actually use the exact same model prosthetic foot!
Maybe we could talk about experiences, problems, advantages...
Recognizing and relating to those who use the same kit: That would be the rare case where the labels and brands make some sense.

For example: My foot requires frequent maintenance bc I wear out the bushing in the ankle torque element pretty fast. Would like to know if other users experience the same problem.
As a prosthetic's user, do you feel like you're part of NASCAR with labeling and advertising?

I have a an idea that can get me hate 

You lose warranty if you were to remove logos from prosthetic limb products

Well that is what differentiates products from companies. I get that you feel weird about it, but let’s say (hypothetically) you are walking down the street with your leg and you meet an amputee who would be able to walk, but just doesn’t have a prosthetic device. It would be difficult to memorize where each part of your leg came from, so having the labels might not just be for the companies. I don’t know the first thing about owning prosthetics so please correct me if I’m wrong, but the company logo might not just be for the companies.

WI.name: Wish / future desire
top_examples:
I will gladly paint your prosthetic pieces. Drawing only what you want then painting it. Or using my own creativity. I have survived cancer three times and am currently dying of kidney failure
But I am still so grateful to be at live. The ticking noise sucks but not all the time. Only shared my stuff because it is my bona fides.peace keep these videos up.they show us no one's weird because we look different.

I amputee in left leg under knee joint.
 I need to get like that 🥺

My prosthetic team told me to paint over the logos if I want on my leg parts.  But your right those are tattoos I didn’t agree to. Love your post started following you a year before I lost my left leg. I love how you explain things my hubby gets it after hearing your post. Thank you

The average person one might meet and see that logo is probably not in the market for that product.  If one is in the market for a prosthetic, one is already in touch with doctors, prosthetists, all the people already in the industry.  Brand recognition seems to me like it should be a low priority in such a specialized field.

😅 As if these need advertising!? "Wanna loose a limb? Get your prosthetic HERE!" 😂🤦‍♀️🤦‍♀️
I would so write over that. Make funny pictures out of this.
But..your ankle... actually looks like your signature. Like a F and a J in one. Footless Jo.😂 Which I just saw, cos my sister's name starts with a J and her new husband's name starts with an F and they have a similar sign edged into their wedding rings 😅
